file_name: Sahu et al. - 2025 - Detection of non-melanoma skin cancer by deep convolutional neural network and stochastic gradient d.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN) for segmentation and classification of non-melanoma skin cancer, along with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for feature extraction and Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) for optimization, utilizing the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and testing of a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimization for detecting non-melanoma skin cancer. It utilizes the HAM10000 dataset, performs feature extraction using PCA, and reports specific quantitative results (98.57% accuracy) from their proposed model, distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: DETECTION OF NON-MELANOMA SKIN CANCER BY DEEP CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK AND STOCHASTIC GRADIENT DESCENT OPTIMIZATION ALGORITHM
journal: Journal of Mechanics of Continua and Mathematical Sciences
brief_summary: This study proposes an automatic detection system for non-melanoma skin cancer using deep learning techniques to assist dermatologists. The authors utilized the HAM10000 dataset, employing a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) for both image segmentation and classification tasks. Feature extraction was performed using Principal Component Analysis (PCA), and model optimization was achieved via the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) algorithm. The evaluation approach involved training and validating the model over 100 epochs, comparing its performance against previous literature. The proposed model achieved a classification accuracy of 98.57%, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary focus of the study is the detection and classification of non-melanoma skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. The paper explicitly states in the title and methodology that it concentrates on non-melanoma cancer.

Melanoma is mentioned in the context of the HAM10000 dataset (which contains melanoma images) and in the novelty section regarding the ability to detect melanoma or non-melanoma. However, the specific experiments and classification tasks described in the methodology and results focus on non-melanoma detection. The paper references other works (e.g., Rezvantalab et al.) that focused on melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, but does not report specific experimental results for melanoma classification in its own proposed model.

Mentioned only in the Related Work section when citing Rezvantalab et al. [XXII], who focused on melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. Not reported as a specific target class for the experiments conducted in this paper.

all_task_types.target: Non-melanoma skin cancer detection (binary: cancerous vs. non-cancerous tissues)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is the 'detection of non-melanoma skin cancer' and 'classification of non-melanoma'. The methodology involves a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) for segmentation and classification. The results report classification accuracy (98.57%) and F1 scores, indicating a discrete diagnostic category prediction task.

all_task_types.target: Skin biopsy images (identifying cancerous and non-cancerous tissues)
all_task_types.details: The methodology section lists 'Segmentation by DCNN' as a step, and the results mention that 'cancerous along with non-cancerous tissues have been identified' via segmentation. However, the primary performance metric and final output focus on the classification accuracy of the detection task.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesion images obtained from skin biopsy tests, accessed via Kaggle. The study utilized a subset of this dataset containing 10,015 data points (images) for the proposed model.

use.training: Used for training the Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) model with data augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation of the dataset during the learning phase.
use.testing: Used for testing purposes to evaluate classification accuracy and F1 scores.
use.other: Used for feature extraction via Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and demographic analysis of 4,460 cases (age, sex, diagnosis stage).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Feature extraction and dimensionality reduction of the HAM10000 dataset.
Optimization of the Deep Convolutional Neural Network model by minimizing the objective function during training.
Model training and performance evaluation over 100 epochs to track changes in accuracy and F1 score.

statistical_methods_used.method: Confusion Matrix-derived Metrics (Precision, Recall, F1 Score, Accuracy)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Quantitative evaluation of the classification model's performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and F1 scores over 100 epochs, and compared qualitatively to previous literature without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment: Proposed DCNN with PCA and SGD Optimization
HAM10000.notes: The proposed model achieved the highest accuracy (98.57%) and F1 score (82.32%) among the compared methods. The study focused on non-melanoma skin cancer detection. Segmentation results identified cancerous and non-cancerous tissues.

architecture_details.objective_function: J = sqrt(delta_w + 1) / sqrt(delta_b) (where delta_w and delta_b are Eigen Values Associated with the Linear System, w is weight and b is bias)

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation by Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) to isolate cancerous and non-cancerous tissues from skin biopsy images
Feature extraction using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction
Data augmentation to create new learning sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to create new learning sets from the existing dataset to avoid negative impacts on model performance due to noise. The paper mentions using data augmentation techniques generally but does not specify the exact geometric or color transform methods, probabilities, or ranges employed.

conclusions: The proposed model, which integrates Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN), Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for feature extraction, and Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) for optimization, achieved a classification accuracy of 98.57% on the HAM10000 dataset.

The proposed method demonstrated superior performance compared to previous literature, including CNN, ResNet 152, and other deep transfer learning models, which reported accuracies ranging from 74.76% to 95%.
The model effectively segmented skin biopsy images to identify cancerous and non-cancerous tissues, aiding in the automatic detection of non-melanoma skin cancer.

The study suggests that the proposed deep learning approach can assist clinical experts and dermatologists in early diagnosis and prescribing correct treatment, potentially improving patient survival rates.
The authors note that future improvements in classification accuracy could be achieved by utilizing larger datasets, microwave integrated circuit chips, and addressing imbalanced classes.
important_remarks: The study focuses on non-melanoma skin cancer detection but utilizes the HAM10000 dataset, which is heavily biased towards melanoma and other benign lesions, potentially limiting the generalizability of the model for non-melanoma classes.

The paper does not report the specific train/validation/test split ratios or the exact number of samples per class used in the experiments, preventing assessment of data leakage or class imbalance handling.
Data augmentation techniques are mentioned as being used to create new learning sets, but the specific geometric or color transformation methods are not detailed.

The comparison with previous literature is qualitative and based on reported accuracies from other studies rather than a rigorous statistical significance test or re-evaluation of baselines on the same split.

The authors acknowledge in the conclusion that the model's performance could be improved by addressing imbalanced classes and using larger datasets, indicating a limitation in the current dataset's sufficiency.

The objective function for the Stochastic Gradient Descent optimizer is described with a non-standard formula involving eigenvalues, which lacks standard context or justification compared to typical loss functions like cross-entropy.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about clinical applicability to diverse populations or imaging devices.
